coredata.dc:title: Pattern-based recognition of proteins by an array of fluorescent carbon-nanodot receptors
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yulin
Hu, Shun
Zhang, Guoli
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
Liu, Xue
coredata.dc:description: Protein types and concentrations in human body fluid environment have a direct correlation to health conditions, and can be used for primary screening and diagnostics of a great variety of diseases. Herein, we synthesized a series of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) with various ratios of β-cyclodextrin doping, which can recognize proteins and adjust self-emission by combining both the cavity recognition effect of cyclodextrin and noncovalent bonding effect of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups. The difference in β-cyclodextrin contents of various CDs will result in a differential response to various proteins, based on which a sensor array by four CDs was constructed to detect and discriminate six proteins with various subunit numbers. In order to improve the discriminative effect of the sensor array, CD-sensitive metal ions (Cu2+ and Fe3+) were introduced as sensing media to increase the action sites between the CDs and proteins, and these proteins were well discriminated by the CD@metal ion sensor array with a detection limit of 50 μM. The sensor array could work in physiological conditions (such as urine and serum) and realize multiple proteins detection. Combining with the good repeatable detection results and higher sensitivity, the sensor array based on the CDs and metal ions provides an efficient and accurate method to detect proteins, which is important for disease diagnosis through assessing protein levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein recognition
Carbon nanodots
Sensor array
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protein detection and discrimination by CD sensor array
Application of the CD-based sensor array

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s XPS spectra of the CDs.
The TEM images and fluorescent spectra of the CDs.

A) The fingerprints of the proteins (50 μM), B) the scatter diagram of the proteins (50 μM), C) the fingerprints of the proteins (100 μM), and D) the scatter diagram of the proteins (100 μM) generated by the CDs sensor array.

A) The fingerprints, B) the scatter diagram, and C) The fingerprints and D) the scatter diagram of the proteins (50 μM) generated by the CD@Fe3+ sensor array. E) the dendrogram of the proteins (50 μM) generated by the CD@Cu2+ sensor array.

A) Double-blind experiments; B) and C) the scatter diagrams of the proteins generated by the CD@Cu2+ sensor array in imitated serum and urine samples, respectively; D) the scatter diagram of CAT and BSA at different molar ratios. All these experiments were carried out in a total protein concentration of 50 μM by the CD@Cu2+ sensor array.

head.ce:title: Pattern-based recognition of proteins by an array of fluorescent carbon-nanodot receptors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Protein types and concentrations in human body fluid environment have a direct correlation to health conditions, and can be used for primary screening and diagnostics of a great variety of diseases. Herein, we synthesized a series of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) with various ratios of β-cyclodextrin doping, which can recognize proteins and adjust self-emission by combining both the cavity recognition effect of cyclodextrin and noncovalent bonding effect of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups. The difference in β-cyclodextrin contents of various CDs will result in a differential response to various proteins, based on which a sensor array by four CDs was constructed to detect and discriminate six proteins with various subunit numbers. In order to improve the discriminative effect of the sensor array, CD-sensitive metal ions (Cu2+ and Fe3+) were introduced as sensing media to increase the action sites between the CDs and proteins, and these proteins were well discriminated by the CD@metal ion sensor array with a detection limit of 50 μM. The sensor array could work in physiological conditions (such as urine and serum) and realize multiple proteins detection. Combining with the good repeatable detection results and higher sensitivity, the sensor array based on the CDs and metal ions provides an efficient and accurate method to detect proteins, which is important for disease diagnosis through assessing protein levels.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and efficient detection of proteins in serum, urine and saliva provides essential pathological conditions for the early diagnosis of many diseases such as cancers, Alzheimer's disease (AD), prostatitis, and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) [1–3]. Human body fluids contain nearly 10 000 different proteins with an overall protein concentration of 1 mM; the concentration of a single protein can reach more than 100 g/L or only less than 1 ng/L [4]. The relative or absolute level of these proteins is directly related to specific disease states [5].

State-of-the-art detection methods of proteins are constantly emerging, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), western blotting, molecular imprinting, mass spectrometry, gel electrophoresis, and fluorescence analysis techniques [6–10]. Some of these methods have already been adopted in medical diagnosis, but most of them are far from application due to a series of limitations, such as complicated preparation methods, large-scale instrument dependence or time-consuming operation sequence. Additionally, most of these methods are focused on the analysis of a specific protein and cannot deal with a group of proteins especially in physiological conditions, while accurate diagnosis can be accomplished with the help of multiple proteins analysis in most cases. Therefore, new analytical techniques of proteins with convenient construction methods, reduced operation sequence and multivariate analysis ability needs to be urgently exploited and utilized.

Chemical “nose/tongue” technique based on cumulative signals from a group of sensors can realize the simultaneous determination of multiple analytes [11]. Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) sensor array has been developed as a new chemical “nose/tongue” approach for protein analysis, which provides an alternative and preferable solution instead of traditional “lock–key” specific protein analysis methods [12,13]. Recently, Luo et al. synthesized CDs by mixing P2O5 and various amino acids, and these CDs could be applied to construct sensor array for protein detection [14]. In addition, NH2-rich CDs were developed by Rotello et al. as protein-responsive probes and grouped into sensor array for the detection and identification of multiple proteins [15,16]. All of these literatures confirm the practicality of CDs as the sensor of a sensor array, but the detection limit and operability of these array analysis systems for proteins based on CDs are insufficient for actual applications.

Herein, we constructed a sensor array by using a CD with special functional groups, β-cyclodextrin. β-Cyclodextrin is a compound made up of seven glucose molecules bound together in a hollow truncated cone cyclic structure, which gives it the ability to accommodate hydrophobic molecules [17]. It has been reported that β-cyclodextrin can stabilize protein through its own cavity structure [13].The β-cyclodextrin-based CDs were synthesized by directly using β-cyclodextrin and glycine as carbon source. By regulating the mole ratio of β-cyclodextrin and glycine, a series of CDs with various β-cyclodextrin contents were designed and put together into a sensor array to realize the detection of multiple proteins. In order to enhance the detection limit of CD-based sensor array for proteins, metal ions were added as transferring media to improve the interaction between the CDs and proteins. Metal ions could interact with the CDs and proteins through coordination or electrostatic effect; the resulting metal-protein complex or metal-induced deformed proteins would also affect their initial positions on the CDs, such as within or without the hydrophobic cavity. All these actions would lead to a far richer fluorescent signal output and a solid detection result. Besides, the addition of metal ions would not only enhance the acting force between sensors and analytes, but increase the combination rate, which finally create a more stable, more sensitive and more universal sensing system. The multi-touch (cross-reactive) sensor, also entitled identification-generating probe (ID-probe), can generate unique identification patterns for members of specific protein families. Principal component analysis (PCA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) were applied in this sensing system to analyze sensing results, construct sensing models and image sensing data. The sensing system can not only work in extensive physiological environments, such as serum and urine, but also produce repeatable and accurate experimental results in double-blind experiments. To sum up, a simple and low-cost sensor array-based sensing system was constructed to realize convenient, quick, accurate and reproducible analysis of multiple proteins in complicated physiological environments.

Cytochrome c (CYT C, purity≥95%), catalase (CAT, 5000 units/mg protein, quality level elite), lysozyme (LYS, ultra pure grade), trypsin (TRY, tissue culture grade), and bovine serum albumin (BSA, molecular biological level) were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co, Ltd. (China). Human serum albumin (HSA, purity≥99%) was obtained from Berseebio Technology Co, Ltd. (China). Bovine serum, β-cyclodextrin and glycine were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co, Ltd. (China). Anhydrous ethanol, NaOH and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. (China). All the purity of proteins was higher than 95%. Ultrapure water was used in all synthetic experiments. All the detection experiments were carried out in 0.1 M Phosphate-Buffered Saline (PBS, including NaCl 137 mM, KCl 2.7 mM, Na2HPO4 10 mM, and KH2PO4 2 mM), which was purchased from Corning Co, Ltd. (US). All the solvents were of analytical grade and used as received.

Simulated serum samples were prepared according to the active ingredients of fetal bovine serum, and contained a standard solution and real fetal bovine mixed by the ratio of 9:1. The standard solution was made up of albumin 31 g/L, alkaline phosphatase 206.5 u/L, cholesterol 0.34 g/L CaCl2 0.051 g/L, NaCl 8.72 g/L, KCl 1.31 g/L, triiodothyronine (T3) 120 ng/L, thyroxine (T4) 16 μg/L, NaH2PO4 0.71 g/L, triglycerides 0.87 g/L, tetracycline 1 μg/L, uric acid 0.46 g/L, glucose 1.62 g/L, and bilirubin 50 mg/L. Simulated urine samples were prepared with NaCl 3.2 g/L, KCl 5 g/L, CaSO4 0.45 g/L, MgSO4 0.385 g/L, NaH2PO4 0.31 g/L, CO(NH2)2 18.6 g/L, Na2SO4 4.2 g/L, and C4H7N3O 1.1 g/L. The fetal bovine serum was purchased by Lonsera Co, LLC. (Uruguay). T3 and bilirubin were purchased in Sigma-Aldrich Co, LLC. (US). Cholesterol, alkaline phosphatase, tetracycline, and T4 were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co, Ltd. (China). The other reagents in the simulated physiological environment tests were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. (China).

Fluorescence measurements were executed on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU, Japan). Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded to use a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) tests were applied to observe the morphology of the CDs and carried out on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. The fluorescence lifetime was measured by using Fluorolog (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a TCSPC system.

β-Cyclodextrin and glycine of different ratio (mole ratio = 4:66, 3:67, 2:68, 1:69 = A-CDs, B-CDs, C-CDs, D-CDs) were applied to prepare the CDs via using one-pot microwave method. Firstly, β-cyclodextrin and glycine were dissolved in 10 mL 0.2 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution. And then, the solution was heated in microwave oven for 4 min. The resulted CD solid powder was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 4 min and then dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48 h to remove the excess precursors. The maximum absorbance of the preliminarily obtained CD solution was adjusted to 0.1 and then kept in refrigerator at 4 °C.

A series of stock solutions of each protein were prepared firstly for the sensing experiments. The concentrations of these protein solutions were calibrated through absorbance measurements at 280 nm and calculated by using the Beer-Lambert law (C = A 280 /(ε
280 • L)). For the initial detection experiments, 2 mL of the CD solution was loaded into a cuvette and the fluorescence intensity at their optimal emission wavelength was measured via excitation with their optimal excitation wavelength to obtain satisfactory detection results. This fluorescence intensity was recorded as the initial fluorescence intensity, I 0. Subsequently, 10 μL of solution with or without Cu2+ at an adequate concentration was added, and then 20 μL of protein solution with an adequate concentration was added into the former solution with or without Cu2+. After uniform mixing by a vortex agitator, the final fluorescence intensity was recorded immediately as I. All the experiments were performed at room temperature (298 K). Protein detection in simulated physiological environment was carried out directly by applying simulated serum and urine to replace previous PBS solution.

The formation of β-cyclodextrin modified CDs can be confirmed from the test results of XPS, FT-IR spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra. XPS can discern the existing surficial elements as well as their chemical states on the CDs (Fig. 1 & Fig. S1). There are just three kinds of elements on the surface of these CDs, including carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which indicates that these CDs were formed basically from the initial carbon sources and nearly without any impurity. The oxygen content of the A-CDs is higher than that of the other three CDs, indicating a higher β-cyclodextrin doping amount for the A-CDs. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra show the main peak at 284.4–288.5 eV, corresponding to sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon atoms existing on the CDs and confirming the formation and composition of the CD skeleton. The signals at higher binding energy (BE) indicate some CN and/or CO species inserting in the samples after microwave [18]. The FT-IR spectra also exhibit the characteristic absorption bands of the graphene skeleton of the CDs, including the stretching vibration of sp2 C–H (2925 cm−1), sp3 C–H (2853 cm−1), C–O–C (3435 cm−1) and CC/CN (1635 cm−1) (Fig. S2A). The absorption peaks at 330 nm and 440 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S2B) further confirm the n−π* transitions and π−π* transitions of the conjugated CO, CN and the aromatic sp2 structure [19,20]. Meanwhile, it can be found that more glycine carbon source will benefit for the formation of the CDs, which can be seen from the gradually dominant π−π* transitions from A-CDs to D-CDs. The high-resolution O1s reveal that oxygen groups had been reserved in the CD structure, and most of these oxygen elements present in hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups. Comparatively, hydroxyl groups is more reserved, from which we can speculate that some of β-cyclodextrin molecules could have been retained on the surface of the CDs in the bottom-up synthesis process. Accordingly, a higher feed ratio of β-cyclodextrin to glycine matches along with a higher ratio of hydroxyl groups to carboxyl groups. The existence of β-cyclodextrin can also be tracked from the strong peaks (C–OH) in the FT-IR spectra of the CDs typically featured around 1020 cm−1 [21,22]. The oxygen-containing functional groups can affect not only the photoluminescence of the CDs but also their binding effect to proteins [23–26]. Besides, the residual amino groups will increase with the decrease of β-cyclodextrin, which might also influence the binding effect of the CDs to proteins.

The morphology of the CDs are basically uniformly spherical from the TEM images, with an average grain diameter of 2–4 nm, and they hardly change with the contents of β-cyclodextrin (Fig. 2 ). However, their photoluminescence behavior varies according to β-cyclodextrin contents (Fig. 2). The optimum excitation wavelengths of A-CDs, B-CDs, C-CDs and D-CDs are 370 nm, 380 nm, 380 nm and 390 nm, respectively, while their relevant optimum fluorescent emission peaks are located at around 440 nm, 450 nm, 460 nm and 470 nm. In short, a weak blue shift appears along with the increase of β-cyclodextrin. It has been reported that the chemical and optical properties of CDs are directly correlated with its functional groups and defects on the surface [27,28]. The introduction of β-cyclodextrin would weaken the oxidation degrees of the CDs through esterification reaction with the exterior carboxyl groups of the initial CDs. A less carboxyl contents or lower oxidation degree is closely related to the blue shift of the photoluminescence of many reported CDs[29,30]. The fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of four CDs have no obvious discrimination (see supporting Table S1), and there are several possible reasons for these findings: several competing deactivation pathways within the CD particles; (2) a broad dispersion of recombination rates for the same radiative deactivation process; or (3) simultaneous luminescence from different CD particles [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protein detection and discrimination by CD sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: These selected proteins are important for both physiological activities and biomedical diagnosis. Serum proteins in the organism account for more than 60 % and have the capacity of maintaining osmotic pressure [33]. The abnormal amounts of LYS in urine can act as the proof of tubular necrosis, chronic nephritis, acute mononuclear leukemia, and other diseases [34,35]. TRY is actually present in very small amounts in the serum of normal people, but when acute pancreatitis occurs, the trypsin level increases significantly [36]. CYT C can catalyze reduction of O2 in vivo and is an important biomarker for judging mitochondrial apoptosis [37]. CAT is a nonspecific indicator for determining pancreatitis and metabolic disease [38].

First of all, four CDs were grouped into a sensor array to directly detect and discriminate CYT C, CAT, LYS, TRY, BSA and HSA. The fingerprints of these proteins are shown in Fig. 3 , and each protein has its own identification (ID) information, which is constituted by a group of unique intrinsic fluorescent signals. The fluorescence quenching or recovering variation could be ascribed to distinct binding modes of various proteins onto the surface of various CDs, further affecting the surface energy state and fluorescence emission output of the CDs. The multidimensional response pattern (4 sensors × 6 protein × 6 trails) generated by the sensor array in the presence of six proteins was statistically analyzed through PCA to achieve a visual scatter diagram for various protein samples (Fig. 3). Each point in the scatter diagram represents one protein sample, and the distance between points represents the specific differences between various protein samples. Based on the differentiated structure of the CDs and proteins, as well as their specific interactions, the sensor array based on the CDs can discern most of these proteins. Especially when the concentration of the proteins increases from 50 μM to 100 μM, both the discriminative power and stability of the sensor array for proteins are significantly enhanced.

The sensing system was constructed by utilizing the size effect of the β-cyclodextrin cavity to capture the subunits of proteins, as well as the binding effect between the CDs and proteins through electrostatic and coordination interaction. Among these chosen proteins, LYS, CYT C, and TRY are monosubunit proteins constructed by only one peptide chain, while CAT, BSA, and HSA are multimeric proteins with more peptide chains. Commonly, multimeric proteins have a larger size compared with monosubunit ones, so these proteins can be firstly classified into three groups based on their size differences (Tetrasubunit Group: CAT, Trisubunit Group: BSA and HSA, Monosubunit Group: LYS, CYT C, and TRY). With a larger size and more subunits, multimeric proteins also have more functional sites, and hence they are easier to be discerned between two kinds of multimeric proteins, even for BSA and HAS with extremely similar protein structure. As for monosubunit proteins, CYT C, TRY and LYS are all monosubunit proteins with similar sizes and isoelectric points. They can be distinguished just with the help of fluorescence inner-filter effect (FIFE). Besides tryptophan, tyrosine, and phenylalanine with endogenous fluorophores, the porphyrin rings in CAT and CYT C could also affect the photoluminescence through FIFE [39,40]. The UV–Vis absorption peak of both CAT and CYT C is located at 405–410 nm, which can just overlap with the fluorescence spectra of the CDs (Fig. S3). The FIFE is especially obvious for CYT C, and all of the CDs show a remarkable fluorescence quenching phenomenon when adding CYT C. Therefore, CYT C can be separated from TRY and LYS by the CD-based sensor array.

In order to further increase the sensitive and discriminative power of the sensing system, metal ions were introduced to play the role of the sensing media. CDs have been reported in a number of literatures about their function of metal ions analysis, and the as-prepared β-cyclodextrin modified CDs were confirmed to show specific response for Cu2+and Fe3+ (Fig. S4). It has been reported that the hydroxyl group in β-cyclodextrin can form special chelates with Cu2+[41,42], so the CDs are relatively more sensitive to Cu2+ with the increase of surficial β-cyclodextrin functional groups. The D-CDs has less cyclodextrin composition, and their sensitivity to Cu2+ was less than the other CDs. The Fe3+ responsiveness of the CDs stems from the coordination effect of the phenolic hydroxyl groups to Fe3+ in the carbon framework [43]. Meanwhile, metal ions can also chelate with the amino acid residues of proteins, deform the structure of proteins and increase the unfolding fragments of proteins, which could finally make proteins show more active sites to interact with the CDs. Besides, the metal ion-induced deformation of protein subunits could also have significant influence in their entrance into the cavity of β-cyclodextrin. Briefly, the addition of metal ions can help build bridges between the CDs and proteins, and effectively activate the whole sensing process. The concentration of metal ions was chosen around the inflection points of their titrations to the CDs to obtain a maximum detection range and reproducibility [44]. When 50 μM Cu2+ was added into the sensing system, all of these six proteins (50 μM) can be well discriminated, and there is no overlapping phenomenon between two protein regions in the scatter diagram (Fig. 4 A&B). Likewise, the introduction of 50 μM of Fe3+ could also produce good discriminative results (Fig. 4C&D). When the concentration of proteins was decreased from 50 μM to 30 μM, the protein differential effect of the sensor array was obviously worse, indicating that the possible detection limitation of the CD@metal ion sensor array to proteins is 50 μM.

Furthermore, hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) was applied to investigate the sensing performance of the sensor array and structural similarity of these proteins (Fig. 4E). The dendrogram of k-nearest neighbour can show a more visualized result than the scatter diagram from PCA [45]. Similar to HCA, six proteins can be well discriminated from the dendrogram of HCA. Monosubunit proteins (LYS and TRY), tetrasubunit proteins (BSA and HSA), and trisubunit proteins (CAT) are grouped respectively, while CYT C, a monosubunit protein FIFE effect with the CDs compared with LYS and TRY, is separated separately. BSA and HSA have nearly 80% sequence homology and 76% three-dimensional structure similarity [46,47]. They have not only very similar structures but also physiological functions. The most striking difference between these two proteins is that there is only one tryptophan residue (Trp 214 at subdomain IIA) in the HSA structure, while two ones (Trp 135 at subdomain IIB and Trp 214 at subdomain IIA) in the BSA structure [48]. Trp 214 participates in the construction of the hydrophobic loop of both BSA and HSA, which can be applied to bind drugs or recognize chirality. Trp 135, a kind of hydrophobic amino acid, is more exposed to a hydrophilic environment and enhance the hydrophobicity of the BSA subunit. Therefore, it can be seen from the sensing system that A-CDs with more β-cyclodextrin play a main role in the discrimination between BSA and HSA by the use of the hydrophobic cavity effect of β-cyclodextrin. TRY and LYS cannot be differentiated directly by the CD sensor array without metal ions. The introduction of metal ions can activate the functional groups both on the CDs and proteins, and enhance the discriminative power of the sensor array. From the previous structural analysis of the CDs, the functional groups on the CDs mainly originate from two carbon sources and the reaction between them. β-Cyclodextrin and glycine could compete with each other to some extent for the positions of the CDs, and more β-cyclodextrin residues will result in less carboxyl groups and amino groups from glycine. The diversify of functional groups of the CDs will help them to interact with metal ions, increase action sites for proteins, and improve sensitivity of sensing system, so this is consistent with the observation in the CD sensor array with metal ions that D-CDs play a key role in the discrimination between TRY and LYS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CD-based sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: All of the potential practical applications were investigated based on the CD@metal ion sensing system. First of all, double-blind experiments were carried out to evaluate the stability of the sensor array (Fig. 5 A). The above-mentioned six proteins were randomly selected with the concentration ranging from 45 to 55 μM. It is satisfying that all the chosen proteins match well with their established areas. Such experiments can also be applied to detect a given protein through pre-modeling, or compare structural similarity between two proteins.

It is very important for direct protein detection in physiological environment or crude biological samples. Therefore, detection experiments with interferents were carried out in imitated serum and urine samples, which presents a complicated environment. Blood test is invasive test with a high concentration of intrinsic proteins, on the other hand, urine test is non-invasive with a relatively low concentration of proteins and high concentration of inorganic salts. No matter in serum samples or urine samples, 50 μM of proteins can be well distinguished (Fig. 5B. C&Fig. S6A. B).

Proteins in physiological environments are important diagnostic indicator for a great variety of diseases. In order to achieve accurate diagnosis result, multiple proteins indexes need to be combined to be detected and evaluated in most cases[49]. For this purpose, BSA and CAT were mixed and prepared with five different concentration ratios. Notably, all of these samples can be well identified without any overlap, demonstrating the excellent recognition capability of the sensor array for protein mixtures (Fig.5D&Fig. S6C). All these above-mentioned results confirm the practicability of the CD-based sensor array for analyzing protein samples with complicated ingredients.

A novel fluorescent sensing system, sensor array based on β-cyclodextrin-CD and metal ions, was developed for detecting and discriminating proteins. The recognition was realized through two ways: the hydrophobic cavity of β-cyclodextrin can stabilize the subunits of proteins; the surficial functional groups (such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups) of the CDs can act with amino acid residues through coordination or electrostatic effect. By adjusting the ratios of β-cyclodextrin on the CDs, the interaction between the CDs and proteins could be regulated and a differential fluorescence output from the CDs came into being. Metal ions were able to activate the functional groups both on the CDs and proteins, and promoted the interaction between the CDs and proteins. In previous sensor array systems for protein detection, the adopted sensors were generally fluorescent dyes, fluorescent proteins or metal nanoparticles, which are either high-priced or non-environmental. CDs are promising candidates for the construction of sensor array for proteins due to their low-cost eco-friendly sources and simple convenient preparing methods. Compared with the existing sensor array for proteins based on CDs, the current sensing system is more time and labor-saving to construct with a reasonable low detection limit (50 μM) for protein detection. Especially, the sensor array has presented a robust detecting results for proteins even in imitated serum and urine samples. Therefore, the current work is prospective on developing diagnostic methods against specific diseases through monitoring protein biomarkers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873085), Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province of China (20180510023 and 20180550947), Program for the Top Young and Middle-aged Innovative Talents of Higher Learning Institutions of Liaoning Province of China (LR2018010), Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (LJC201906) and Shenyang Municipal Program for the Top Young and Middle-aged Innovative Talents of Liaoning Province of China (2017kjjzqn04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

See supporting information for the detailed information including: the supplementary characterization information of the CDs (including the XPS, FT-IR, UV–Vis spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and fluorescence quantum yields of the CDs), the UV–Vis spectra of CAT and CYT C, the responsiveness of the CDs to metal ions, and the supplementary experiments about protein detection by using the CD@metal ion sensor array. This material is available free of charge via the Internet at the Website.
Multimedia component Multimedia component

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120551.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Abnormal cytokinesis in cells deficient in the breast cancer susceptibility protein BRCA2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal peptide of HIV envelope protein impacts glycosylation and antigenicity of gp120

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensing of proteins in human serum using conjugates of nanoparticles and green fluorescent protein

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A molecular-imprint nanosensor for ultrasensitive detection of proteins
